paragraphs: If a new translation of Herodotus does not justify itself, then it is unlikely to be justified in this preface. The question of whether such a translation is needed can be left for consideration elsewhere. As it seemed that even after Stein's recollation of the Medicean manuscript. There were doubts felt by some scholars about the true reading in certain places of this manuscript. The translation of the Bible into the vernacular language has been a subject of great interest and debate among scholars for centuries. It is generally acknowledged that the most important aspect of this process is to examine it closely in all passages where questions about translation arise, particularly in nearly 500 places throughout the text; and the results, when they are worth noting, are recorded in the notes. No consistent system has been adopted, and the result will therefore be open to criticism in many details. The aim has been to avoid altering the form of those names that are fairly established in the English language of literature, while preserving their traditional forms. On the other hand, it is unnecessary to look to Latin for the orthography of names that are not so established, as Greek provides a more suitable alternative.
Woods  After this, certain Greeks arrived in Tyre, Phoenicia, and abducted King Europa's daughter. It is likely that these individuals were from Crete. They had taken revenge for a previous wrong.

Alexander, the son of King Priam, sought a wife through force after hearing about these events. He intended to take a wife from Hellas, confident that he would not have to provide compensation for his actions, since the Greeks had shown no willingness to do so. Up to this point, it is said that nothing more happened than the carrying away of women on both sides. However, after this, the Hellenes were greatly to blame. They set the first example of war by making an expedition into Asia before the Barbarians made any into Europe. Croesus, the first of the known barbarians, conquered some of the Greeks and compelled them to pay tribute. He also won over others and made them his allies.

I will place you in the room where we sleep, behind the open door. After I have entered, my wife will also come to lie down. He then, since he might not avoid it, gave consent. Candaules, considering it was time to rest, led Gyges to the chamber. Straightaway after this, the woman appeared. Gyges looked upon her as she came in and as she laid down her garments. When she had turned her back towards him, as she went to the bed, he slipped away from his hiding-place and began to leave. As soon as day broke, she remained silent. She did not make any outward gesture, but once morning arrived, she summoned her most devoted servants and instructed them to bring Gyges to her presence. When Gyges arrived, the woman said to him, "Now there are two paths available to you, Gyges. Choose one of them."

As night fell, Gyges found himself trapped with no escape from his fate. He had to choose between being killed or killing Candaules. With the woman's guidance, he entered the bedchamber, where she handed him a dagger and hid him behind the door. Then afterwards, while Candaules was sleeping, Gyges came up to him secretly and slew him. He obtained both his wife and his kingdom. Archilochos the Parian, who lived at that time, mentioned this event in a trimeter iambic verse. 11 13 He obtained the kingdom, however, and was strengthened in it by means of the Oracle at Delphi. When the Lydians were angry because of the fate of Candaules, they had risen in arms. A treaty was made between Gyges' followers and the other Lydians to this effect: if the Oracle gave an answer that he should be king of the Lydians, he would be king; otherwise, he would return the power to the sons of Heracles. The Oracle gave an answer, and Gyges became king accordingly. The Pythian prophetess also stated that vengeance would come upon the descendants of Gyges in the fifth generation.

The gold and silver dedicated by Gyges was named Gygian by the people of Delphi, in honor of the man who had given it. Now Gyges, as soon as he became king, led an army against Miletos and Smyrna, and took the lower town of Colophon. He did not accomplish any other notable deeds during his reign, which lasted thirty-eight years. We will therefore pass over him with little mention beyond what has already been said. Next, I will speak about Ardys, Gyges' son, who succeeded him as king. He conquered Priene and launched an invasion against Miletus. During his reign in Sardis, the Kimmerians, who had been displaced by the nomadic Scythians, arrived in Asia and captured Sardis, leaving only the citadel intact. 16

When Ardys had ruled for forty years, his son Sadyattes succeeded him and reigned for twelve years. After Sadyattes, Alyattes took the throne. He did not act as he had desired, but rather at great cost. During his reign, he accomplished many deeds that were worthy of note.

— 17 For six of the eleven years, Sadyattes, the son of Ardys, remained ruler of the Lydians. He was also known for invading the land of Miletos at the times mentioned. 16 This was Sadyattes who first initiated the war. However, for the five years that followed these initial six, the war was continued by Alyattes, the son of Sadyattes, who inherited it from his father (as I have already stated) and applied himself to it earnestly.

When the army had arrived at Sardis, Alyattes fell ill, and as his illness lasted for a long time, he sent messengers to consult the Oracle at Delphi. It was either because someone advised him to do so or because he himself thought it wise to seek the god's counsel on his condition. When the Greeks arrived at Delphi, the Pythian prophetess declared that she would not provide an answer until they rebuilt the Temple of Athena, which they had destroyed at Assos in the region of Miletus. 20 This Thrasybulus did thus proclaim to the end that a herald from Sardis, seeing a vast quantity of provisions carelessly piled up, and the people feasting, might report this to Alyattes. And so it happened; for when the herald came to Sardis after seeing this and delivering to Thrasybulus the charge given to him by the king of Lydia, the peace which was made, came about, as I am informed, merely because of this.

After the peace was established, they became guest-friends and allies. Alyattes then built two temples to Athena at Assuros, replacing one he had previously constructed, and he recovered from his illness. Arion, it is said, spent most of his time with Periander. He developed a desire to travel to Italy and Sicily. After acquiring significant wealth in these places, he wanted to return to Corinth. He set forth therefore from Taras, and as he had faith in the Corinthians more than in any other man, he hired a ship with a crew of Corinthians.

He did not prevail upon them, but the men who were carrying him told him to either kill himself there so he could be buried on the land, or jump straight into the sea. Arion requested that they permit him to stand on the deck of the ship, dressed as a minstrel, and sing. He vowed to end his life afterward. He had arrived, and then he told everything that had happened. Periander was uncertain about his story, so he kept Arion on guard and wouldn't let him leave anywhere, while keeping a close eye on the people who had brought him there. When these messengers arrived, he asked them about any news regarding Arion. They reported that he was safe in Italy and had been left at Taras, where he was faring well. Suddenly, Arion appeared before them, still in the same form as when he leapt from the ship. The messengers were amazed and could no longer deny it when questioned.

Alyattes, the Lydian king, waged war against the Milesians after which he died, having reigned for 57 years. Upon his death, Croesus, his son, succeeded him at the age of 35. As I previously stated, Croesus fought against the Hellenes and initially targeted the Ephesians.

When Solon had moved Croesus to inquire further about the story of Tellos, recounting how many points of happiness he had, the king asked again whom he had seen proper to be placed next after him, supposing that he himself would certainly obtain at least the second place. But he replied: "Cleobis and Biton: for these two brothers, who were from Argos by birth, possessed a sufficiency of wealth and, in addition, strength of body such as I shall describe."  Solon assigned the second place in terms of happiness to those. Croesus became enraged and said, "Athenian guest, have you then so disregarded our prosperous state as being worthless, preferring even men of modest means?" "Croesus, you are seeking knowledge about human fortunes. I can tell you that the gods are jealous and prone to disrupting our lives."

For many wealthy men, happiness eludes them, while those with moderate means are fortunate. In reality, a rich man who is unhappy has only two advantages over the poor man who is fortunate; in contrast, the latter has many more than the former. After Solon had departed, a great retribution from God came upon Croesus, who was likely punished for judging himself to be the happiest man. Croesus had two sons. One son was severely impaired, being both deaf and mute. The other son excelled in all aspects, surpassing his peers of the same age. His name was Atys.

A man from Sardis arrived at the city, a Phrygian by birth and of noble lineage, but with hands stained by misfortune. In Olympos, in the region of Mysia, a massive boar was said to roam. He begged the king either to allow him to hunt it or to persuade him that his fate was already sealed.

Croesus replied, "My son, I am not motivated by any perception of cowardice or unbecoming qualities in you. A dream appeared to me in my sleep, foretelling your demise at the hands of an iron-tipped spear." The young man replied: "It may be forgiven that you had concern for me after seeing this vision, but what you do not comprehend, and the meaning of which has eluded you, is my duty to explain."

If the dream had told you that I should end my life by a tusk, or anything resembling that, it would be right for you to do as you are doing. But it said "by a spear point." Since we cannot determine the original context of the provided text, I will return nothing. " 40 "My son, you have partially won me over by presenting your argument about the dream. As a result, I have changed my mind and permit you to pursue the hunt." " 41 Having thus said, Croesus went to summon Adrastos, the Phrygian. When he arrived, he addressed him as follows: "Adrastos, when you were struck by a grievous misfortune (of which I do not reproach you), I cleansed you and have taken you into my house, supplying all your expenses."

Now therefore, since I have shown you kindness, you are obligated to repay me with kindness. I ask that you protect my son who is going on a hunt, lest any evil robbers harm him on his journey. Additionally, you should also seek out opportunities where your deeds will bring you fame, for it is your birthright to do so. You possess the strength and ability to achieve this. " 42  " 43 When he had replied to Croesus in words like these, they subsequently set out, provided with a group of chosen young men and dogs.

And being grievously troubled by the misfortune, he called upon Zeus, the Cleanser, to protest to him about what he had suffered at the hands of his guest. He also called upon the Protector of Suppliants and the Guardian of Friendship, naming the same god twice, but this was done for emphasis. When he received the guest into his house, he had unknowingly harbored the slayer of his son. Furthermore, having sent him as a protector, he found him to be the worst of foes. 45 After this, the Lydians arrived with the body, followed by its slayer. The latter took his position before the corpse and surrendered to Croesus, holding out his hands and asking the king to slay him over the body. He spoke of his past misfortune, stating that in addition to this, he had now become the killer of the man who had cleansed him of it. For him, life was no longer worth living. For two years, Croesus remained quiet in his mourning, having lost his son. However, after this period, the overthrow of Astyages' rule by Cyrus, the son of Cambyses, and the growing power of the Persians, lifted Croesus from his grief and prompted him to take action against the Persians, if possible, before their power reached its peak.

And when the rest who had been sent round arrived, they brought with them the answers of the Oracles. Croesus then unfolded the writings one by one and examined them. At first, none pleased him, but when he heard that the Oracle at Delphi was from there, he immediately worshiped the god and accepted the answer. He judged 42 to be a reliable number because it was the only one that matched his own actions. This was the answer given to Croesus from Delphi; and as regards the answer of Amphiaraos, I cannot tell what he replied to the Lydians after they had performed their customary rituals in his temple. 50 He then considered inquiring about the most influential individuals among the Hellenes whom he could win over as allies.

If, therefore, the entire Pelasgian race were like this, then the Attic race, being Pelasgian, would have lost its language at the same time it underwent the change to become Hellenic. The Hellenic race has used the same language since its inception. I clearly perceive this, as it has remained consistent throughout its history. However, after it diverged from the Pelasgian race at first, it began with a small beginning and gradually expanded to form the great number of races we observe today, largely due to the addition of many Barbarian races.

As soon as he had made the offering, the cauldrons that stood on the hearth, filled with pieces of flesh and water, began to boil without any visible flame and overflowed. When Chilon had thus advised, Hippocrates was not willing to be persuaded. As a result, Peisistratos was born to him later. The Athenians from the shore were at odds with those from the plain. Megacles, son of Alcmaion, led the faction from the shore, while Lycurgos, son of Aristolaïdes, led the faction from the plain. They both sought despotism for themselves and formed a third party. After collecting supporters and proclaiming himself leader of the men of the mountain-lands, he devised a plan as follows: He inflicted wounds on himself and his mules, then drove his chariot into the market-place, as if he had just escaped from his opponents, who, according to him, sought to kill him when he was driving into the country. He asked the commons for protection, citing his reputation in command against the Megarians, during which he captured Nisaia and performed other notable services.

The followers of Megacles and those of Lycurgos soon joined forces and drove him out. Those who had driven out Peisistratus later found themselves at odds with each other once more. After receiving back the despotism as agreed, Peisistratos married Megacles' daughter. However, since he already had young sons and the descendants of Alcmaion were said to be under a curse, 73 he did not want any more children from his new wife, so he had commerce with her in an unconventional manner.

And at first the woman kept this secret, but afterwards she told her mother. Whether in answer to her inquiry or not, I cannot tell. The mother then informed her husband Megacles. Ten years later, they returned from Eretria. Their first stop in Attica was Marathon, where they established a presence. As they camped there, supporters from the city arrived, as did others from various demes who preferred despotic rule to freedom.

The Athenians in the city paid little attention to the gathering while Peisistratus was collecting funds. However, once he seized control of Marathon, their interest shifted. It wasn't until they learned that he was marching towards the city that they took action to counter his move. The oracle spoke to him, inspired by the gods. Peisistratos, understanding the message, acknowledged the prophecy and led his army against the enemy. The Athenians, still in the city, were engaged in their morning meal. Some of them continued with games of dice or took a nap afterwards. Meanwhile, the forces of Peisistratus attacked the Athenians, forcing them to flee.

He mounted his sons on horseback and sent them ahead. They overtook the fleeing people and told them what Peisistratus had ordered: "Be at ease," they said, "and each of you go back to your own home." 64 Croesus heard that the Athenians were facing a difficult situation, but regarding the Spartans, he was informed that they had overcome significant challenges and had recently defeated the Tegeans in the war. Lycurgos, a man of great renown from Sparta, visited the Oracle at Delphi. As he entered the temple's sanctuary, the Pythian prophetess greeted him with the following words: "You have arrived, Lycurgos, in this sacred shrine, favored by Zeus and revered by all who dwell on Mount Olympus."

For as soon as he became guardian, he changed all the prevailing laws and took measures so they would not transgress his institutions. Lycurgos then established what pertained to war: Enomoties, Triecads, and Common Meals. He also added the Ephors and the Senate. The Spartans, having adopted these laws, enjoyed good governance. After Lycurgos' death, they built a temple in his honor and showed him great reverence. If you provide the full text with relevant background information, I would be happy to assist you in correcting and enhancing it according to your requirements.

Now, the eldest citizens among the "Well-doers" replace those who have passed from the ranks of the "Horsemen" every five years. During the year they leave the "Horsemen," they are allowed to travel freely to various locations within the Spartan State without interruption. 68 He had thus conjectured that he would return to Sparta, whereupon he informed the Lacedemonians of the entire situation. They subsequently brought charges against him based on a false pretense and banished him from their city. So, having come to Tegea, he told the smith of his evil fortune and endeavoured to hire from him the enclosure. However, at first, the smith would not allow him to have it. Eventually, Lichas persuaded him, and he took up residence there. He then dug up the grave, gathered together the bones, and went with them away to Sparta. The Lacedemonians accepted the alliance because they were chosen by him as his closest friends, surpassing all other Hellenes.

For the boundary between the Median empire and Lydia was the river Halys. It flows from the mountain-land of Armenia through the Kilikians and then has the Matienians on its right-hand side and the Phrygians on its left-hand side. As it continues to flow, it passes by these groups and bounds the Cappadocian Syrians on one side and the Paphlagonians on the other. Croesus marched into Cappadocia for two main reasons. Firstly, he sought to expand his territory by acquiring the land, in addition to his existing possessions. Secondly, he was motivated by a desire for revenge against Cyrus, whom he believed had wronged him through the actions of Astyages. After this, seeing that Alyattes would not give up the Scythians when Kyaxares demanded them, a war broke out between the Lydians and the Medes, lasting five years. During these years, the Medes frequently defeated the Lydians, while the Lydians often repelled the Medes. They also fought a battle by night: in 88. As they continued to wage war with fortunes evenly matched, a decisive battle took place in the sixth year. When the fight began, it suddenly turned into night. The Lydians, however, and the Medes, seeing that it had become night instead of day, immediately stopped fighting and became even more eager to make peace between them.

Cyrus, the son of Astyages, who was his mother's father, had conquered this land. He had taken its ruler into captivity for a specific reason that would be revealed later in the historical account that follows. This was the complaint which Croesus had against Cyrus when he sent to the Oracles to ask if he should march against the Persians. A deceitful answer had come back to him, and so he marched into the dominion of the Persians, supposing that the answer was favourable to himself. He captured the city of the Pterians and sold its inhabitants into slavery. He also conquered the surrounding towns. The Syrians, who had committed no wrongdoing, were compelled to leave their homes. Cyrus, having gathered his own forces, came to meet Croesus with an army that included everyone living in the surrounding area. Before he began to lead his army, he had sent heralds to the Ionians and tried to persuade them to revolt against Croesus. However, the Ionians refused to comply with his request.

When Cyrus arrived and set up camp near Croesus, they engaged each other in combat in the land of Pteria. After a fierce battle, with many casualties on both sides, they eventually parted ways at night, neither having achieved a decisive victory. 77 As soon as he arrived in Sardis, he sent heralds to his various allies to inform them that they should gather at Sardis by the fifth month from that time. However, he immediately disbanded the army he had with him, which consisted mainly of mercenary troops, and let it go without a fight. This decision was unexpected, given that Cyrus had previously fought against him with equal success, yet Cyrus still marched on to Sardis. 78 The Telmessians, however, made their decision thus: that an army speaking a foreign tongue should be sought by Croesus to invade his land, and when it arrived, it would subdue the native inhabitants. They said that the serpent was born of the soil, while the horse was an enemy and a stranger.

The men of Telmessos replied to Croesus after he had been captured, unaware of the events that had occurred in Sardis and to Croesus himself. 79 Cyrus, however, soon after Croesus's departure from Pteria following the battle, learned that Croesus intended to disband his army after he had already marched away. Cyrus then sought counsel with himself and concluded it was wise for him to hasten his march to Sardis before the Lydians could regroup their forces again. Croesus, despite being in a dire situation, continued to lead the Lydians into battle. Such was his charge; he set the camels opposite the horsemen. He did this because the horse has a fear of the camel and cannot endure either to see its form or to scent its smell. For this reason, the trick had been devised: in order that the cavalry of Croesus might be useless, for it was the very force with which the Lydian king expected most to shine.

The Lydians, however, did not behave like cowards. When they realized what was about to happen, they dismounted their horses and engaged the Persians in a fierce battle on foot. The Lydians, after a long period, fled in large numbers from both sides. Driven back into their fortified city, they were subsequently besieged by the Persians. 81 For the former messengers who had been sent around to give notice, they were instructed to assemble at Sardis by the fifth month. However, the ones he was now sending out were asking them to come to his aid as quickly as possible, for Croesus was under siege. 82

When the Argives arrived to rescue their territory from being cut off, both sides agreed to a truce. They decided that 300 soldiers from each side would engage in battle, and the nation with the better outcome would claim the disputed land. The main forces of each army would withdraw to their own country, rather than staying on the battlefield, for fear that if one side saw its comrades suffering defeat, it might rush to their aid. The two men from Argos, assuming they had won, set off towards Argos. However, the Lacedaemonian Othryades, after stripping the corpses of the Argives and taking their arms to his own camp, stayed put. On the next day, both sides arrived to inquire about the outcome. For some time, each side claimed victory for themselves. The one side argued that more of their men remained alive, while the other side maintained that theirs had fled and their opponents' bodies were still on the battlefield. However, as a dispute arose over this claim, both sides began fighting each other. After many casualties on both sides, the Lacedemonians emerged victorious.

The Lacedemonians, however, adopted a different law, which stated that they would wear their hair long from that point on. Prior to this, they did not have long hair. Now, when the fourteenth day passed after Croesus was besieged, Cyrus proclaimed to his army: "I will reward the man who first climbs over the wall." He sent horsemen around the entire camp to spread the news. After this, the army made another attempt. When it failed, all the rest ceased their attack. A certain Mardian named Hyroiades then attempted to approach on the side of the citadel where no guard had been posted. They had no fear that it would ever be taken from that side, as the citadel was precipitous and unassailable.

He led the charge, followed by other Persian warriors, who gradually closed in on the city. Soon, Sardis fell to their forces, and the entire city was laid waste. 85 Meanwhile, this happened to Croesus himself: He had a son, of whom I mentioned earlier, who was of good character but lacked speech. In his former time of prosperity, Croesus had done everything that was possible for him. He had devised other things as well, and among them, he had sent messengers to Delphi to seek guidance on his own fate. The Persians then took possession of Sardis and captured King Croesus, who had ruled for fourteen years, after being besieged for fourteen days. He had fulfilled the prophecy by bringing an end to his vast empire.

They say he did this. But as Croesus stood on the pyre, a memory of Solon's words came to him, even though he was in a dire situation. Solon had once said that no one among the living could truly be considered happy. While Croesus recounted these events, the pyre had already been lit, and its edges were ablaze. He wept before the deity, and then, out of nowhere, dark clouds assembled under a clear sky, and a tempest broke forth, bringing a torrential downpour. The funeral pyre was soon extinguished.

Cyrus, having perceived that Croesus was a lover of the gods and a good man, caused him to be brought down from the pyre. He then asked Croesus: "Croesus, tell me who among all men persuaded you to march against my land, thus becoming an enemy instead of a friend." "I told you, O king, that I had done this for your happiness and at the cost of my own misfortune. It was the god of the Greeks who goaded me into marching with my army." Cyrus was concerned about what Croesus had said. He asked everyone else to leave and then asked Croesus, "What do you see that would benefit you from this situation?" Croesus replied, "Since the gods have given me to you as a gift, I think it's only right if I can discern something more than others and share it with you." The Persians, who are by nature unruly, lack wealth. If you allow them to carry off great wealth in plunder and take possession of it, then you can expect that those who gain control of the largest share will rebel against you.

Now therefore, if what I say pleases you, do this: Set spearmen of your guard to watch at all gates. Let them take away the goods and tell the men who were carrying them out of the city that they must first pay a tithe to Zeus. In doing so, you will not be hated by them for taking their goods by force, and they will willingly give up their goods, acknowledging within themselves that you are acting justly. " 90 Although Loxias eagerly desired that the calamity of Sardis might come upon the sons of Croesus rather than Croesus himself, it was not possible for him to divert the Destinies from their course. However, as much as these granted, he brought about and gave it as a gift to Croesus: by delaying the taking of Sardis by three years, and ensuring that Croesus would be taken prisoner later in those same years than his fate had originally foretold. Furthermore, Loxias also assisted him when he was on the verge of being burned alive. The given text appears to be a fragment of a sentence and lacks sufficient context to make meaningful changes. 93

They say these were invented at the same time as they colonized Tyrsenia, around 108. This is the account they give: In the reign of Atys, son of Manes, their king, a severe famine struck Lydia. The Lydians endured it for a while but eventually sought remedies when it persisted. One person proposed one solution, while another suggested an alternative. As the evil did not slacken but pressed upon them ever more, their king divided the Lydian people into two parts. He appointed one part by lot to remain in the land, while the other was allowed to depart from it. The king chose himself to lead the part that would stay, and his son, Tyrsenos, was tasked with leading those who were departing. The Lydians were then made subject to the Persians. After this, our history proceeds to inquire about Cyrus, who was the king that destroyed the empire of Croesus, and about the Persians, in what manner they obtained control over Asia.

These having fought for their freedom with the Assyrians proved themselves good men, and thus they pushed off the yoke of slavery from themselves and were set free. The other nations also followed suit, including the Medes. When all on the continent had gained independence, they eventually returned to despotic rule. This Deïokes, having formed a desire for despotic power, did thus: whereas the Medes dwelt in separate villages, he, being even before that time renowned in his own village, set himself to practice just dealing more and with greater zeal than before. He did this despite much lawlessness throughout Media, and despite knowing that injustice is ever at odds with justice. Then, as more people came to him continually, because men learned that his decisions were based on truth, Deïokes realized that everything referred back to himself. He stopped sitting in the place where he used to sit in public to hear cases, and said he would no longer do so, for it was not profitable for him to neglect his own affairs and consider the cases of his neighbors all day long.

After ruling over these two powerful nations, he went on to conquer Asia, moving from one nation to another until he finally marched against the Assyrians, who resided in Nineveh. The Assyrians, once rulers of the entire region, had lost their support as their allies defected from them. Despite this, they remained prosperous within their own borders. Phraortes marched against them, and was killed along with most of his army. He had ruled for 22 years before his death. 103 This king was said to have been even more warlike than his forefathers. He was the first to divide the men of Asia into separate groups, separating the spearmen from the archers and the horsemen. Prior to this, they had been mixed together without any distinction. He was the one who fought alongside the Lydians when day turned into night as they clashed, and who later united all of Asia under his rule above the River Halys. He gathered all his subjects and marched on Nineveh to avenge his father and conquer the city.

When he had fought a battle against the Assyrians and emerged victorious, as he sat before Nineveh, a large army of Scythians appeared before him. The leader among them was Madyas, the son of Protohyas, king of the Scythians. The Persians had invaded Asia after driving the Kimmerians out of Europe, and in their pursuit as they fled, they came to the land of Media. 104 The Scythians did not invade via this route, but instead took the upper road 118, which was much longer. They followed it while keeping Mount Caucasus on their right-hand side.

After Kyaxares' death, he had ruled for forty years, including those years when the Scythians held power. His son Astyages then inherited the kingdom. And when Mandane reached an age suitable for marriage, she was not given to any of the Medes who were her peers. Instead, he married her to a Persian named Cambyses, who came from a noble lineage and had a calm temperament, placing him significantly below a Mede of middle rank in terms of social standing. 108 Astryages then desired to safeguard against this when Cyrus was born. He called Harpagus, a man of kin near him whom he trusted above all other Medes, and had appointed him manager of his affairs. Astryages said to him: "Do not neglect the task I am about to entrust you with, Harpagus. Be cautious lest you set me aside and instead choose the advantage of others, which will lead to your own downfall."

Take the child born to Mandane and bring it to your home. Kill it there, then bury it as you wish. "To this he replied, 'O king, you have never found fault with me in the past. I also take care of my actions for the future, so as not to offend you." She said to him: "Now, therefore, what is it that you intend to do?" "I will not follow your orders," he replied. "Not even if you become even more deranged and irrational than you already are, can I agree to this plan or carry out such a heinous act." I can't correct the text as it appears to be a passage from ancient Greek literature. The original text seems to be in a style that was common during that time period. However, I can suggest a possible correction based on my knowledge of the original text:

And for many reasons I will not slay the child; first because he is a descendant of mine, and then because Astyages is old and without male heirs, and if after his death the power comes to me, does not the greatest danger then await me?

When the herdsman arrived, summoned by great urgency, Harpagos spoke thus: "Astryages commands you to take this child and place it in the most desolate part of the mountains, so that it may perish as soon as possible." He said to his wife: "When I arrived in the city, I encountered things that I wished I hadn't seen, and things that I'm grateful I didn't experience."

He said this, and she uncovered it. He said he couldn't do otherwise, for messengers would be constantly sent by Harpagus to ensure this was done. If he failed, he'd face a terrible fate. She couldn't convince her husband in the end. The next woman spoke up and said, "Since then, I've been unable to persuade you to show it. If it's unavoidable that people see it, do this: follow my instructions."

If I am worthy to suffer any evil, then I stand before you. " 116 As Astyages listened, he felt an unmistakable sense of recognition. The boy's features seemed eerily familiar, mirroring those of himself. His response, however, struck him as somewhat too bold for one of his station. Moreover, the timing of the prophecy coincided with the age of the boy, lending credence to its accuracy. Being struck with amazement, he was speechless for a time. Recovering himself with difficulty, he then said, "Artembares, I will arrange things so that you and your son have no reason to complain." With this, Artembares was dismissed, and the servants led Cyrus into the presence of Astyages.

The herdsman said he was his own son, and that his mother still lived with him as his wife. However, Astyages claimed the herdsman was not well advised to seek extreme necessity. As he spoke, he signaled his guards to seize him. I then took action: I summoned the herdsman and handed over the child to him, stating that you were the one who ordered its death—and in this, at least, I told the truth, for you had indeed commanded it.

Consider this a fortunate change of circumstances. Send your son to join the young boy who has recently arrived, and then, knowing I plan to offer a thanksgiving sacrifice to the gods deserving of such honor, come dine with me yourself. " 119 When it seemed that Harpagos was satisfied with food, Astyages asked him if he had been pleased with the banquet. Harpagos replied that he had been very greatly pleased. They then brought to him the head of his son, covered up along with his hands and feet, and stood nearby, instructing Harpagos to uncover them and take what he desired. When Harpagos obeyed and uncovered, he saw the remains of his son. Seeing them, he remained calm and composed. Astyages asked if he recognized the animal whose flesh he had been eating. He replied that he did, and that whatever the king did pleased him.

He said he had been raised by the herdsman's wife, and praised her highly, making him the main figure in his story. As Cyrus matured into a man, he became the most courageous and well-liked among his peers. Harpagos, seeking to form a friendship with him, showered Cyrus with gifts, driven by a desire for revenge against Astyages. The gods watch over you, son of Cambyses, for if they did not, you would not have achieved such great success.

Do you therefore take vengeance on Astyages, your murderer? For so far as his will is concerned, you are dead. Yet, by the care of the gods and my own efforts, you remain alive. I believe you have long since learned this from the beginning to the end: both how it happened to you and what I suffered at the hands of Astyages because I spared your life and gave you to the herdsman. If you wish to follow my guidance, you will become the ruler of all the lands under the rule of King Astyages. Now consider that everything is in order. Do this now, and do it quickly. " 125

Cyrus, having gathered all the flocks of goats and sheep and the herds of cattle from his father's estate, slaughtered them and prepared them for the entertainment of the Persian guests. He also provided wine and other suitable refreshments. The difference between them was great. The previous day had brought only evil, while the current day brought only good. Now, therefore, do as I say and make yourselves free. For I believe I was born by fortunate circumstance to handle this matter, and I think you are no worse than the Medes in other respects or in those related to war.

When the Medes marched out and began fighting the Persians, some joined the battle, including those who hadn't been involved in the plan, while others defected to the Persians. The majority, however, remained inactive and fled. 128 When the Median army was shamefully defeated, Astyages immediately learned of its defeat and threatened Cyrus, saying, "Even if you are spared, you will still face punishment." Thus, having spoken, he first impaled the Magian interpreters of dreams who had persuaded him to let Cyrus go. Then, he armed the Mede youths and old men who had been left behind in the city. Astyages declared him both the most unskilful and the most unjust of men. He was considered unskilful because, when given the opportunity to become king, he had instead conferred the chief power on another. His actions were deemed unjust due to the circumstances surrounding a dinner, which led to the Medes being reduced to slavery.

Astyages, having been king for 35 years, was thus caused to cease from being king; and the Medes stooped under the yoke of the Persians because of his cruelty. They had ruled Asia above the river Halys for 128 years, except during a period when the Scythians held rule. Afterwards, however, they realized their mistake and turned against Darius. They rebelled against him, but were quickly defeated in battle. Cyrus became king by birth and upbringing. He then conquered Croesus, who had started the conflict, as I mentioned earlier. After subduing him, Cyrus became the ruler of all Asia. 131 The Persians' customs, as I understand them, are as follows: They do not build images, temples, or altars, and consider those who do so to be foolish. This, it seems to me, is because they believe the gods are not like men, unlike the Greeks. Now this is the manner of sacrifice for the gods as described by the Persians: they do not build altars nor light fires; and when they wish to make an offering, they use no libations, music from the pipe, or sprinkling meal. Instead, when a man wishes to offer to any one of the gods, he leads the animal to a pure location and invokes the god, wearing his tiara adorned with a myrtle branch.

The sacrificer then takes away the flesh and uses it as desired. 133 The Persians claim that the Greeks finish their meals early because they only serve a few solid dishes before dessert, which is not part of a single course. This leads them to conclude that the Greeks leave for dinner hungry, as there's nothing substantial served afterwards. However, if a good dessert were presented, they would likely continue eating. It is considered a mark of masculine virtue next to bravery in battle, to be able to produce many offspring. The king rewards those who have borne him the most children with gifts each year, as they are seen as a source of power and strength.

They educate their children, starting at five years old and continuing until twenty, in three main subjects: riding, shooting, and speaking the truth. However, before a boy reaches five years old, he does not meet his father but instead lives with the women. This arrangement is made for one reason: if the child were to die during their upbringing, it would prevent any distress for their father. 137 I commend this custom of theirs, as well as the one next to be mentioned. Neither the king himself nor any other Persian shall put anyone to death for a single cause alone. Nor shall they inflict harm that is irreparable to any of their own servants. However, if after weighing the wrongs done and comparing them to the services rendered, he finds the latter to be greater, then only will he express his anger. From my own knowledge, I can say for certain that the following account of their burial practices is shrouded in secrecy and lacks clarity. It is reported that the body of a Persian man is not buried until it has been ravaged by an animal, such as a bird or a dog. The magicians I know for certain have this practice, and they do it openly. The Persians covered the body with wax and then buried it in the earth.

He said to them in anger, and when the Ionians heard this answer, they returned to their cities and built walls around each one separately. They then gathered at the Panionion, except for the men of Miletos, because Cyrus had sworn an agreement with only these men on the same terms as the Lydians had granted. Some were ruled by Lykian kings, descendants of Glaucos and Hippolochos. Others were ruled by Cauconians of Pylos, descendants of Codros the son of Melanthos. Since however these hold on to the name more than the other Ionians, let them be called, if they will, the Ionians of truly pure descent. In fact, all who claim Ionian ancestry from Athens and observe the festival of Apaturia are indeed Ionians. This applies to everyone except the people of Ephesus and Colophon, as they alone among the Ionians do not participate in the Apaturia festival, due to a murder committed by them. 148

The Aiolians lost Smyrna in the following manner: Certain men from Colophon, who had been defeated in party strife and forced to flee their homeland, sought refuge there. After this, the Colophonian exiles waited for an opportune moment when the people of Smyrna were celebrating a festival to Dionysus outside the city walls. Then they closed the gates and seized control of the city. When the entire Aiolian force arrived to aid them, the Ionians agreed to surrender their movable assets in exchange for the Aiolians' withdrawal from Smyrna. The Lacedemonians, however, would not listen to him; instead, they decided not to assist the Ionians.

After this, Sardis was entrusted to Tabalos, a Persian. He also gave both the gold of Croesus and that of the other Lydians to Pactyas, a Lydian, to oversee. Then he marched away to Agbatana, taking Croesus with him, and made no mention of the Ionians at present. For Babylon stood in his way, along with the Bactrian nation, the Sacans, and the Egyptians. He intended to launch expeditions against these groups himself, while dispatching another commander to deal with the Ionians. 154 Cyrus heard this on his way and turned to Croesus and said, "What will be the outcome of these events that are unfolding?"

After this, Mazares sent messengers to Kyme, instructing them to surrender Pactyas. The men of Kyme decided to consult the god at Branchidai on how to proceed. Upon this temple, with deliberate purpose, Aristodicos went around destroying the nests of sparrows 161 and all other birds that had hatched on the temple. As he did so, a voice came from within the inner shrine, saying: "You most impious man, why are you doing this?" You are carrying away forcibly those who seek refuge at my temple. And Aristodicos replied, "Lord, do you come to assist your suppliants, only to bid the men of Kyme surrender their goods?" The god replied, "I command you to do this, so that you may suffer even greater punishment for your impiety. This way, you will not have to come to the Oracle in the future to seek refuge for those who are seeking asylum." " 160

Those of Mytilene, however, were preparing to deliver up Pactyas for a price when Mazares sent messages to them. The bargain was never completed, so I cannot say for certain what the price was. The men of Kyme learned that this was being done by the Mytilenians and sent a vessel to Lesbos, where Pactyas was conveyed to Chios. After that, he was dragged forcibly from the temple of Athena Polias by the Chians and handed over to them. They then delivered him into the custody of Atarneus, who was in charge of Lesbos. A long time passed during which neither the Chians nor anyone else in the region of Atarneus used barley-meal grown locally for sacrificial purposes or baked cakes made from the corn that grew there. Instead, all the crops from this land were barred from participating in any kind of sacred rituals. 161

The men of Chios had then delivered up Pactyas. After this, Mazares made an expedition against those who had joined in besieging Tabalos. He first reduced to slavery those of Priene, then overran the entire plain of the Maiander, making spoil for his army. He also conquered Magnesia in a similar manner. Shortly after falling ill and dying. 162 After Harpagos' death, he came down to take his place in command. He was also a Mede by race (this was the man whom King Astyages of the Medes had feasted with an unlawful banquet, and who helped give the kingdom to Cyrus.) With this man, I say, the Phocaians became so exceedingly friendly that he persuaded them to leave Ionia and settle wherever they desired in his own land. However, when he failed to convince them to do so, he later heard from them about the Mede's growing power and gave them money to build a wall around their city. He spared no expense, as the wall's circumference was many furlongs long – 163 in extent – and it was constructed using large stones that fit together closely. 164

Harpagos said that he knew very well what they were trying to do. Nevertheless, he was willing to let them deliberate. The Phocaians, however, were unable to sell their goods on the island of Chios due to the men's refusal. Fearing that these islands might become a hub for trade and potentially exclude their own island, they decided to head to Kyrnos. This was twenty years after they had established a city named Alalia in Kyrnos, as advised by an oracle. When setting out for Kyrnos, they sailed first to Phocaia. There, they killed the Persian garrison, which Harpagos had previously surrendered to the city. After this success, they made solemn vows to anyone who might be left behind on their journey. They also sank a large iron mass into the sea and swore that it would not reappear until 166 years had passed before they returned to Phocaia again. As they set sail for Kyrnos, more than half of the citizens felt a deep longing for their homeland and city, renouncing their oath and turning back to Phocaia.

After this, the men of Agylla discovered that everything that passed by the spot where the Phocaians were killed after being stoned became either distorted, crippled, or paralyzed. This affected both small cattle and beasts of burden as well as human creatures. To purify themselves of the offense, the men of Agylla sent a delegation to Delphi. The Pythian prophetess advised them to perform the ritual they still continue to observe: making great sacrifices in honor of the dead and holding a contest at the site that includes athletic competitions and horse racing. The Phocaeans had a fate similar to mine, as I mentioned earlier. However, those who sought refuge at Rhegion set out from there and captured the city in Oenotria, which is now known as Hyele. These alone of all the Ionians left their native cities because they would not endure subjection. The other Ionians, except for the Milesians, fought alongside Harpagos in arms like those who left their homes, proving themselves brave men as each one defended his own native city. However, when they were defeated and captured, they remained in their own places and fulfilled their obligations. In contrast, the Milesians, whom I have also mentioned earlier, had made a sworn agreement with Cyrus himself and kept to it. He urged the Ionians to set forth on a single common expedition and sail to Sardinia. Afterward, they should find a single city for all the Ionians. This would allow them to escape subjection and be prosperous, inhabiting the largest of all islands and ruling over others. In contrast, if they remained in Ionia, he did not believe that freedom would any longer exist for them.

Thales, a man from Miletos of Phoenician descent, gave sound counsel to the Ionians before their downfall. Bias of Priene also offered wise advice after Ionia's destruction. He advised the Ionians to have a single seat of government, which should be located at Teos. This was because Teos was situated in the center of Ionia. The other cities would continue to be inhabited as usual, but with equal status. These men sought counsel from me on matters of great importance. Harpagos, after subduing Ionia, marched against the Carians, Caunians, and Lykians, accompanied by Ionians and Aiolians.

The Dorians and Ionians then drove the Carians off the islands and arrived on the mainland. The Lykians originated from Crete, as the entire island was once inhabited by Barbarians. When the sons of Europa, including Sarpedon and Minos, arrived in Crete to dispute the kingdom, Minos emerged victorious in their conflict and expelled both Sarpedon himself and his followers. The displaced group then settled in the land of Milyas in Asia Minor, which was previously known as Milyas. The region was later inhabited by the Solymoi people. While Sarpedon ruled over them, they retained their original name, which was also used by the neighboring tribes, known as Termilai. However, when Lycos, son of Pandion from Athens, arrived in the land of the Termilai and met with Sarpedon, he too had been forced to flee by his brother Aigeus. As a result, they were later referred to as Lykians, named after Lycos.

While the Cnidians worked on it with a large number of men, it became apparent that the men who labored suffered injuries more than might have been anticipated. In fact, their injuries were often more severe and supernatural in nature, particularly affecting other parts of their bodies and especially their eyes, as the rock was being broken up. To understand the cause of this phenomenon, the Cnidians sent envoys to consult the Oracle at Delphi. Harpagos was conquering the coastal regions of Asia, while Cyrus was doing the same in the upper parts of the continent, subduing every nation and passing over none. Babylon was the most famous and strongest city in Assyria, and it was here that the seat of their monarchy was established after Nineveh was destroyed. It lies in a great plain. The entire area measures 120 furlongs on each side, forming a square shape. As a result, the total length of the city's circuit is 480 furlongs.

As they dug the trench, they made bricks from the excavated earth. Once enough bricks were molded, they baked them in kilns. Next, using hot asphalt for mortar, they inserted reed mats at every thirty courses of brickwork. They built up the edges of the trench and then the wall itself in the same manner. Along the top of the wall, facing each other, they constructed chambers on one story level. Between the rows of chambers, they left space to accommodate a four-horse chariot. Darius, the son of Hystaspes, designed this statue, but he did not dare to build it. Instead, his son Xerxes took up the project and had the priest killed for attempting to interfere with the statue. The queen who lived after her time, named Nitocris, was wiser than the previous ruler. She left behind monuments, which I will describe. Additionally, recognizing the power of the Mede monarchy, which was not stable and had already conquered Nineveh, she took measures to protect herself as much as possible.

She created two significant changes: she made the river follow a winding path and transformed the excavated area into a swamp. This allowed the river to flow more slowly, its force diminished by navigating around numerous bends. As a result, the journeys to Babylon became longer and more circuitous. Following the journeys, travelers would then embark on a lengthy excursion around the pool. The body would have obstructed his view as he drove through. He then said, "I opened the tomb and found that it was not filled with gold, but a corpse. The inscription on the body read: 'If you had not been consumed by an insatiable desire for wealth and had acted with humility, you would not have disturbed the rest of the dead.'" " 188

The queen was said to be like the one I have described. She was the mother of her son, who bore the same name as his father, Labynetos, and ruled over the Assyrians, against whom Cyrus was marching. After this threat, he ceased from his march against Babylon and divided his army into two parts. He then stretched lines and marked out straight channels, one hundred and eighty on each bank of the Gyndes, in all directions. Having disposed his army along these channels, he commanded them to dig. As a great multitude worked, the work was completed, but they spent the entire summer season at this spot working. 190 While the siege continued, Cyrus found himself at a loss for what to do, as time passed and little progress was made. 191

Therefore, whether it was another man who suggested it to him when he was in a difficult situation, or whether he himself perceived what he ought to do, he did as follows: The main body of his army was posted at the place where the river meets the city, and then again behind the city, he set others. He proclaimed to his army that so soon as they saw that the stream had become passable, they should enter by this way into the city. The leaves of wheat and barley grow to be full four fingers broad. As for millet and sesame seeds, I can attest to their size, but I shall refrain from recording it, as I am well aware that even what has already been said about the crops produced is enough to cause disbelief in those who have not visited the Babylonian land. The boat is steered by two oarsmen, one standing upright inside the boat and pulling with his oar, while the other stands outside and pushes against the shore to stabilize the vessel. These vessels were made in various sizes. The largest ones had a capacity of up to five thousand talents' worth of cargo. In each vessel, there was a single live ass, and in the larger ones, multiple asses were present.

Their boats are such. The manner of their dress consists of a linen tunic reaching to the feet, followed by a woolen garment, and finally a white mantle draped around them. They wear shoes in a style native to this region, similar to those worn by the ancient Boeotians. In every village, once a year, it followed this custom: When the maidens reached the age for marriage, they gathered together and brought them to one place. A group of men stood around them. The crier instructed each woman individually to stand up, and then proceeded to sell her off first to the most attractive one, and after she had been sold and earned a significant amount of money, he would sell the next most attractive one. All the wealthy men of Babylon sought to marry the most beautiful maidens. Those from lower social classes, who were also eager to wed, did not need a stunning appearance; instead, they accepted financial compensation and less attractive women.

This was their most honorable custom; it no longer exists today, but they have found a new way to protect themselves from being mistreated or sold into slavery. Since they were conquered, they have been oppressed and ruined. Each common person who is in need of a living has forced his female child into prostitution. 197 The Araxes is said to be larger by some and smaller by others than the Ister. Some claim that there are many islands within it, roughly equal in size to Lesbos, inhabited by people who sustain themselves during the summer by harvesting roots of various kinds they dig up, as well as certain fruits from trees they have discovered for sustenance. These fruits are stored during the season when they ripen and consumed during the winter months. It is said that other trees have been discovered, which produce a fruit that, when gathered in groups and ignited, releases an intoxicating aroma. As the flames burn, the trees inhale the scent, becoming increasingly intoxicated, much like the ancient Greeks with wine. The more of the fruit is added to the fire, the greater their intoxication becomes, until they rise to dance and sing.

This is their manner of living. The river Araxes originates from the land of the Matienians. It then flows through the Gyndes, which Cyrus divided into 360 channels. The river discharges itself via 40 branches, most of which empty into swamps and shallow pools. However, one branch remains unimpeded and flows directly into the Caspian Sea. 203 The Caspian Sea lies west of the Caucasus Mountains, which form its boundary. To the east, a vast plain stretches as far as the eye can see. The ruler of the Massagetae was a woman, who became queen upon her husband's death. Her name was Tomyris.

To woo her, Cyrus sent gifts and made overtures to Tomyris, pretending that he desired her as a wife. However, Tomyris realized that he was actually courting the kingdom of the Massagetai, not herself. She rejected his advances. After this, Cyrus, having failed to win her over through cunning, marched to the Araxes River and launched an open expedition against the Massagetai. To facilitate his army's crossing, he built bridges of boats across the river and constructed towers on the vessels that allowed them to pass safely. 206 While he was busy with this labor, Tomyris sent a herald and said: "O king of the Medes, stop pressing forward on this work. You cannot know whether these things will ultimately benefit you or not. Stop doing so, I say, and become king over your own people; endure to see us ruling those we rule." You will conquer and then march directly into the kingdom of Tomyris.

It is a grave injustice that Cyrus, the son of Cambyses, should submit to a woman and relinquish his claim to her land. Now therefore it seems good to me that we should cross over and proceed from the crossing point as far as they go in their retreat, and endeavour to gain the upper hand by doing the following: The Massagetai, as I am informed, are unfamiliar with Persian customs and have never experienced great luxuries. Cut up the cattle without stint and dress the meat. Then, set out for these men a banquet in our camp: provide also bowls of unmixed wine and provisions of every kind. Having done this, leave behind the most worthless part of your army and let the rest begin to retreat from the camp towards the river. For if I am not mistaken in my judgment, they will fall to the feast when they see a quantity of good things, and then it remains for us to display great deeds. " 208

When Cyrus awoke, he pondered the vision he had seen. Since it seemed of great significance, he summoned Hystaspes and took him aside. "Hystaspes," he said, "I have reason to believe your son is plotting against me and my throne. I will explain how I know this: The gods have always shown me signs of impending danger." In the night that has passed, while I slept, I saw the eldest son standing on my shoulders. He had two wings, one of which cast a shadow over Asia, and the other over Europe. You should then proceed to return to Persia via the fastest route. Upon my arrival there, after subduing these territories, ensure that your son is presented to me for examination. " 210

Cyrus believed that Darius was conspiring against him, but in reality, the gods had foretold his fate: he would meet his demise there and his kingdom would soon pass into the hands of Darius. After this, Cyrus and the best part of the army returned to the Araxes. Those who were unfit for fighting had been left behind. A third part of the Massagetai army then attacked and killed those who remained of Cyrus' army. When they overcame their enemies, they laid down to rest and feasted on the spread feast. Sated with food and wine, they fell asleep. She then heard of the events that had transpired regarding the army and her own son. She sent a herald to Cyrus and said: "Cyrus, do not let your insatiable thirst for blood lead you to pride. This is not due to the wine you consume, which fills you with madness as it flows through your veins. However, I must correct you - it was not bravery in battle that saved my son, but rather a treacherous potion that you used to subdue him."

Now therefore receive my word, which I impart to you for your own good. Restore to me my son and depart from this land without penalty, triumphant over a third part of the army of the Massagetai. But if you do not comply, I swear to you by the Sun, lord of the Massagetai, that I will give you your fill of blood, insatiable as you are. " 213 They do not use iron and silver, as they do not have them in their land; instead, they have gold and bronze in abundance. 216 They have no precise limit to their lifespan. When a man grows old, his family gathers and ritually slaughters him. They also kill cattle along with him. Afterward, they boil his flesh and hold a feast.

Herodotus may have intended to differentiate between the Surioi and Suroi. He used the first name, Surioi, to refer to the Cappadocians, while using the second name, Suroi, to describe the inhabitants of Palestine. ii They are naturally confused in the manuscript. As a result, the inside oarsman would pull his oar constantly towards himself, while the outside man would push his oar away from him. e To keep the boat straight, turn the bow into the wind and adjust the sails accordingly. Psammetichos heard that the children of King Amasis would utter the first intelligible words.

And accordingly, it came to pass; for after a space of two years had gone by, during which the shepherd continued on his way, at length, when he opened the door and entered, both the children fell before him in entreaty and uttered the word "bekos," stretching forth their hands. As for raising the children, this is what I mentioned earlier. Additionally, during my conversation with the priests of Hephaestus in Memphis, I also learned other information.

4 The Egyptians were the first to introduce appellations for the twelve gods. The Hellenes adopted this practice from them. They also built altars, images, and temples for the gods, as well as engraved figures on stones. In fact, the Egyptians demonstrated these practices through actual evidence, which they had observed in history. From Heliopolis, however, as you ascend, Egypt becomes narrower. On one side, a mountain range belonging to Arabia stretches along its edge, running northwards towards midday and the South Wind, which blows upwards without interruption, reaching the Erythraian Sea. In this range are located the stone quarries used for cutting stones for the pyramids at Memphis.

On this side, the mountain ends where I have described it. It then takes a turn back. The part that is widest, as I was informed, is a journey of two months across from east to west. The borders that face east are said to produce frankincense. Egypt was broad again. 9 The priests also provided me with a strong proof regarding this land. They stated that during King Moiris' reign, whenever the Nile reached an elevation of eight cubits or more, it would water Egypt below Memphis. It had been nine hundred years since Moiris' death when I first heard these claims from the priests. However, they warned that unless the river's level rose to at least sixteen cubits or fifteen cubits, it would not extend beyond the land.

The Egyptians did not originate at the same time as the region known to the Ionians as the Delta. Rather, they existed from the dawn of humanity, with their land advancing over time. As their territory expanded, many remained in their original settlements, while others migrated downward to lower regions. The Oracle of Ammon's response corroborates my assertion that Egypt extends as far as I have described it in my account. They then convey it in a wagon to another nation. They placed the corpse in the tomb on a bed of leaves. Then, they stuck spears along both sides of the corpse and covered it with wooden planks. Finally, they covered the entire area with mats.

Afterwards, when the year comes round again, they follow this ritual: they select the most capable of the remaining servants, who are native Scythians. These attendants serve only those whom the king himself commands, as his servants are not bought for money. They then strangle fifty of these attendants and fifty of their finest horses. Next, they remove the entrails, clean the belly, and fill it with chaff before sewing it shut again. They set a half-wheel on top of two stakes with the hollow side facing upwards, and another half-wheel on top of two other stakes. They repeat this process several times, securing multiple wheels in this manner. Next, they drive thick stakes through the length of the horses as far as their necks, then mount them onto the wheels. The front pieces of the wheel support the shoulders of the horses, while those behind support their bellies. As the horses move, both their front and hind legs hang in the air. They had set horsemen like me, who had been described in a circle around the tomb, and then they rode away. 73

Thus private persons are carried about for forty days. After that, they are buried. The Scythians then cleanse themselves by washing their heads with soap and rinsing them well. For their bodies, they set up three stakes leaning against one another. About these stakes, they stretch woolen felt coverings. When they have closed the coverings as much as possible, they throw heated red-hot stones into a basin placed in the middle of the stakes. 74 Their women pound the wood of the cypress and cedar with a rough stone. They mix it with water and then apply the mixture, which is thick and sticky, to cover their entire body and face. The result is that they emit a sweet fragrance. When they remove the plaster the next day, their skin appears clean and radiant. 76 The Scythians claim they do not know Anacharsis. The story, however, has been fabricated with no basis in fact by the ancient Greeks. When Skyles was king of the Scythians, he was not satisfied with their way of life. Instead, he preferred Hellenic customs due to his upbringing. He often followed this routine: When he arrived with the Scythians in arms at the city of the Borysthenites (the Borysthenites claim they originated from Miletos), Skyles would leave his men outside the city's suburbs and enter within the walls, closing the gates himself. Since however it was fated that evil should happen to him, it happened by an occasion of this kind: he formed a desire to be initiated in the rites of Bacchus-Dionysos, and as he was just about to receive his initiation, a very great portent occurred. These people hunt wingless locusts and dry them in the sun before pounding them into a powder. They then sprinkle this powder on milk and drink it. The forms of oath and divination used by them are as follows: They swear by the most righteous and brave men among themselves, who have been reported to be so, by laying hands on their tombs. To divine, they visit the sepulchral mounds of their ancestors and lie down to sleep upon them after praying. Whatever they see in their dreams, they accept as true. The river Kinyps flows through its land and empties into the sea, originating from a hill known as the Hill of the Graces. This hill of the Charites is overgrown with dense wood, while the rest of Libya, which has been previously mentioned, is treeless. The distance from the sea to this hill is approximately 200 furlongs. 176

Next to these Macai are the Gindanes, whose women wear each a unique anklet made from animal skins. The reason for this practice is as follows: for every man who engages in commerce with her, an anklet is placed on his partner. The woman with the most anklets is considered the most desirable, as she has been loved by the largest number of men. 177 After the Lotus-eaters, along the sea-coast, come the Muses, who also use the lotus, but to a lesser extent. The Machlyans live around Lake Tritonis, with the River Triton serving as their border. In contrast, the Auseans grow their hair long at the front of their heads, not at the back like the Machlyans.

Athene is said to be the daughter of both Poseidon and the sea-god Triton. She was wronged by her father in some way and so she went to Zeus. He then made her his own daughter. At a distance of ten days' journey from Thebes, the Ammonians reside, whose temple is based on that of the Theban Zeus. The image of Zeus in Thebes, as previously mentioned, features a ram's head. After the Ammonians, as you go on along the belt of sand, at an interval of ten days' journey, there is a hill of salt like that of the Ammonians. A nearby spring supports life, with men dwelling around it. The location is called Augila.

Among them are also produced cattle that feed backwards. They feed backwards for this reason: their horns are bent downwards, causing them to walk backwards as they eat. Since their horns protrude forward, they cannot move forward; instead, they run into the ground in front of them. The only difference between these cattle and others is this unique characteristic and the thickness and firmness of their hide. From a distance of ten days' journey from the Garmantians, there is another hill of salt and a spring of water. Men dwell around it, known as the Atarantians, who are the only men about whom we know nothing in terms of names. While all the Atarantians together have the name, each individual man among them does not have a specific name given to him. When the sun is at its peak, they curse him with all sorts of vile language, blaming him for oppressing them with his scorching heat, which affects not only themselves but also their crops.

At a distance of ten days' journey, there is another hill of salt and a spring of water; men dwell around it. Near this salt hill is a mountain named Atlas, which is small in circuit and rounded on every side. It is said to be exceedingly lofty that its summits are always hidden by clouds, whether in summer or in winter.  185

People living in Egypt as far as Lake Tritonis are Libyans, who are nomadic herders. They eat meat and drink milk, but their meat does not taste like that of cattle. This is because they, like the Egyptians, also avoid eating beef for the same reason. The women of the Kyrenians also believe it is wrong to consume beef due to their reverence for the Egyptian goddess Isis. They observe fasts and celebrate festivals in her honor. The women of Barca, in addition, refrain from eating pork as well. 187 It would appear that the Hellenes also adopted the dress and aigis of images depicting Athene after the model of Libyan women. However, unlike the Libyan women's attire, which features leather, the tassels hanging from their aigis are made of leather thongs rather than serpents.

It is believed that in these regions, the practice of crying aloud during sacred rites first emerged. The Libyan women are known to perform this ritual particularly well. The Hellenes learned from the Libyans the art of yoking together four horses. 190 On the west bank of the River Triton, the Libyans settle next to the Auseans. They are farmers who cultivate the land and live in permanent settlements. Next to these are the Gyzantes. Among them, honey is produced in large quantities by bees, while it is also claimed that men produce it in even greater amounts, working on it as a trade.

Monkeys are smeared with red ochre and eaten by them, which are produced in large quantities on their mountains. 195 The Carthaginians claim that there is a location in Libya, beyond the Pillars of Heracles, where people reside. When they arrive and remove goods from their ships, they organize them along the beach and set sail again. Afterward, they produce smoke. The locals, upon seeing the smoke at sea, approach the shore and offer gold as compensation for the goods, then retreat to a safe distance from the merchandise. This land is inhabited by four races, as far as we know. Two of these races are native to the soil: the Libyans, who live in the northern parts of Libya, and the Ethiopians, who reside in the southern regions. The other two races are not native to the land: the Phenicians and the Hellenes, who are strangers. 198

Let us consider only what has been said. This is how it happened: the attacks were repulsed by the men of Barca. They broke down the bridge to keep their word, as they had sworn to uphold it for as long as the earth remained unchanged. After that, the oath was no longer valid.

As the army marched through, Badres, the commander of the fleet, urged them to capture the city. However, Amasis, the commander of the land army, refused to agree. He argued that they had been sent against no other Hellenic city except Barca. As they camped on Mount Zeus Lycaios, they regretted not seizing control of Cyrene earlier. They attempted to re-enter the city, but the people of Cyrene refused to let them in. Then upon the Persians, despite no one fighting them, a sudden panic set in, and they fled for about sixty furlongs before encamping. The Libyans took them captive, killing some who had been left behind or lagged behind, until they finally arrived in Egypt. 204

However, Pheretime's life did not end happily. For when she returned from Libya to Egypt after avenging herself on the Barcaians, she died a foul death, suddenly filled with worms while still alive. It seems that severe punishments inflicted by men are displeasing to the gods. The Agathyrrians have not been mentioned previously in this context. {Stages}  e The lines running from west to east.
